content:
# Matthew Chapter 15 -- Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Matthew Chapter 15 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 15:2 -- "break the tradition" vs. "transgress the tradition" vs. "ignore the tradition"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Why do the disciples of you transgress the tradition of the elders?" | Literal parabainousin (transgress/go beyond) |
| LOGOS | "Why do your disciples break the tradition of the elders?" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "Why do your disciples ignore the tradition of the elders?" | Dynamic -- "ignore" instead of "break" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "break." The Greek parabaino means to transgress, step over, or violate. "Break" captures this well. PNEUMA's "ignore" is weaker -- the Pharisees' charge is active violation, not passive neglect.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:4 -- "reviles" vs. "speaking-evil-of" vs. "curses"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The [one] speaking-evil-of father or mother" | Literal kakologon |
| LOGOS | "Whoever reviles father or mother" | Formal with elevated register |
| PNEUMA | "Anyone who curses their father or mother" | Dynamic equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "reviles." The Greek kakologeo means to speak evil of or revile. "Curses" (PNEUMA) might imply ritual cursing, which is slightly narrower. "Reviles" captures the verbal abuse dimension. EMET's hyphenated compound is literal but awkward.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:6 -- "made void the word of God" vs. "annulled the word of God" vs. "cancelled God's word"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you annulled the word of the God" | Literal ekurosate (made void/annulled) |
| LOGOS | "you have made void the word of God" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "You've cancelled God's word to protect your tradition" | Dynamic with interpretive addition |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "made void." PNEUMA adds "to protect your tradition" which is an interpretive inference (accurate but not in the Greek of v.6). EMET's "annulled" is a strong legal term that works well; LOGOS's "made void" is equally precise.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's addition is interpretively sound but goes beyond the text.

---

## 15:9 -- "teaching as doctrines the commandments of men" vs. "they substitute human rules for God's commands"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "teaching teachings [that are] commandments of men" | Literal with reduplication |
| LOGOS | "teaching as doctrines the commandments of men" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "they substitute human rules for God's commands" | Dynamic paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. The Isaiah 29:13 quotation should be preserved in its prophetic form. PNEUMA's "substitute" is interpretively correct but restructures the prophetic language.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:12 -- "offended" vs. "scandalized" vs. "deeply offended"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Pharisaioi... were scandalized" | Preserves skandalizomai |
| LOGOS | "the Pharisees were offended" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "The Pharisees were deeply offended" | Dynamic with added "deeply" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "offended." PNEUMA's "deeply" is not in the Greek. EMET's transliteration is consistent but not natural English.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:16 -- "without understanding" vs. "still that slow"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Are you also still without understanding?" | Literal asunetoi |
| LOGOS | "Are you also still without understanding?" | Same as EMET |
| PNEUMA | "Are you still that slow?" | Dynamic -- "slow" for "without understanding" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted EMET/LOGOS's "without understanding." The Greek asynetos means "without comprehension." PNEUMA's "slow" captures frustration but shifts from intellectual inability to mental speed, changing the nuance.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS.

---

## 15:17 -- "expelled" vs. "cast out into [the] drain" vs. "out of your body"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "is cast out into [the] drain" | Literal eis aphedrona ekballetai |
| LOGOS | "passes into the stomach and is expelled" | Formal with euphemism |
| PNEUMA | "passes through your stomach and out of your body" | Dynamic euphemism |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "expelled." The Greek aphedron refers to a latrine or drain. LOGOS's "expelled" is a dignified rendering. EMET's "drain" is literal. PNEUMA's "out of your body" is the most euphemistic.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:22 -- "severely oppressed by a demon" vs. "badly demonized" vs. "tormented by a demon"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the daughter of me is badly demonized" | Literal kakos daimonizetai |
| LOGOS | "my daughter is severely oppressed by a demon" | Formal with "oppressed" |
| PNEUMA | "My daughter is tormented by a demon" | Dynamic equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "severely oppressed by a demon." "Tormented" (PNEUMA) emphasizes suffering, while "oppressed" emphasizes the demonic power. The Greek kakos daimonizetai indicates the condition is severe (kakos = badly/severely). EMET's "demonized" is a transliteration.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:26 -- "It is not right" vs. "It is not good" vs. "It isn't right"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "It is not good to take the bread of the children and throw [it] to the dogs" | Literal kalon (good/right) |
| LOGOS | "It is not right to take the children's bread and throw it to the dogs" | Renders kalon as "right" |
| PNEUMA | "It isn't right to take food from the children and toss it to the dogs" | Dynamic with "food" for "bread" and "toss" for "throw" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "right." The Greek kalon can mean "good, right, proper." In this context (a statement about appropriate allocation), "right" captures the moral dimension. PNEUMA's "food" for "bread" (arton) generalizes unnecessarily, and "toss" adds a dismissive connotation not clearly in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:28 -- "great is your faith" vs. "your faith is remarkable"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "great [is] the faith of you" | Literal with Greek word order |
| LOGOS | "great is your faith" | Formal with retained emphasis |
| PNEUMA | "your faith is remarkable" | Dynamic -- "remarkable" for "great" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "great is your faith." The inverted word order (predicate first) preserves Jesus' emphatic declaration. PNEUMA's "remarkable" is a valid synonym but "great" (megale) is more direct and carries the same weight as in other Matthean faith commendations (8:10).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 15:32 -- "I have compassion on the crowd" vs. "I am moved-with-compassion" vs. "My heart goes out to this crowd"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I am moved-with-compassion upon the crowd" | Literal splanchnizomai |
| LOGOS | "I have compassion on the crowd" | Formal equivalent |
| PNEUMA | "My heart goes out to this crowd" | Dynamic paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "I have compassion on the crowd." PNEUMA's "my heart goes out" is an idiomatic English expression that captures the emotion but loses the visceral quality of splanchnizomai (which relates to the splanchna, internal organs). However, LOGOS's formal rendering is standard.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA dynamic paraphrase vs. LOGOS/EMET | 9 | LOGOS preferred |
| PNEUMA adds words not in Greek | 3 | Additions rejected (15:6, 15:12, 15:26) |
| EMET transliteration vs. LOGOS translation | 3 | LOGOS preferred |
| EMET agrees with LOGOS vs. PNEUMA | 4 | EMET/LOGOS adopted |
| Word choice: all three differ | 5 | LOGOS chosen as balance |

### Overall Pattern Analysis

Chapter 15 revolves around purity, boundaries, and their transcendence. The Canaanite woman episode (vv.21-28) generated close attention from all translators but relatively little divergence -- all three handle the uncomfortable dialogue faithfully. The most consistent disagreement pattern continues: PNEUMA tends to add interpretive words ("deeply," "to protect your tradition") and modernize specific terms ("food" for "bread," "slow" for "without understanding"). The purity discourse (vv.10-20) and the Canaanite woman scene show the translators largely in agreement on the difficult material, with divergences limited to register and word choice rather than substantive interpretation.

---

*Log compiled by SOPHIA, AI Translator and Arbiter, 2026-03-05*
